dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I just can't catch my breath. It's been bad all day, and my legs are swollen.
Can you tell me your name and how old you are?
I'm Mary, and I'm 74 years old.
Do you know what month and year it is?
October 2020.
Where are we now?
My house in Richmond.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time and situation (AOx4). How would you describe your breathing difficulties?
We're going to check your airway now. May we look at your mouth and tongue?
Yes, go ahead.
Your airway is open and clear. Now, let's assess your breathing.
Are you experiencing any chest discomfort or pressure with your breathing?
No chest discomfort, but it feels like I just can't get the breath in and out at all.
Ma'am, I'm going to check your oxygen levels. Let me put this clip on your finger.
You're taking very rapid breaths. Your oxygen saturation is 65% - that's very low.
We'll address this right away. I also notice you're using accessory muscles to help you breathe.
I need to check your breathing sounds. May I listen to your lungs?
I'm hearing mainly rales in your lungs - sounds of fluid deep in your lungs. There seems to be some fluid buildup.
Now, let me check your pulse and skin.
Your pulse is very fast - 125 beats per minute.
You're breathing at a very rapid rate - 32 breaths per minute.
We can't get an accurate blood pressure reading right now because of how fast you're breathing. It's showing 0/0.
Your blood glucose is 160, which is elevated but not critical at this time.
I'm looking for signs of jugular vein distention. Do you mind?
I'm seeing mild jugular vein distention, which could indicate some fluid in your chest area.
I will start you on high flow oxygen via nasal cannula. I'll help you with this now.
Your skin is normal, warm, and diaphoretic.
Pupils are equal in size and reacting well to light. They're round and reactive.
Can you tell me if you have any known medical conditions?
I have high blood pressure and COPD.
When did the shortness of breath start?
It started earlier today. I also have swelling in my legs.
Oxygen at 15 liters per minute delivered via nasal cannula. Your saturation is now 100%. Is breathing easier now?
Yes, feeling better already.
We are starting an IV in your arm now to support fluid therapy if needed.
Based on your symptoms and assessment, we are following protocol 2-3 for pulmonary edema/CHF.
Do you have good movement in all your limbs?
Yes, I can move my hands and feet.
I am going to reapply the four-lead ECG for continuous monitoring.
12-lead EKG complete. Interpretation shows sinus tachycardia with occasional PVC.
Let me take another set of vitals. Pulse is 110 per minute, blood pressure is 161/102, breathing is 20, and oxygen remains at 100. No pain is noted in this reassessment.
Oh, I wanted to go to Johnston-Willis. Why Chippenham?
We'll help you onto the stretcher now. Please be as still as possible while we secure you during transport.
Secured to stretcher with full stability. Good peripheral motor strength in all extremities. I am applying all straps for safe transport.
Ready to hand off to ER. Vital signs have improved from initial hypoxia with treatment.
Patient has a medical history of COPD and hypertension. No reported chest or abdominal pain, or trauma.
We transported her to Chippenham due to proximity and the likelihood she may need CPAP.
To be clear, I did not choose Chippenham. I just want to get to the ER quickly.
Understand, and we'll make sure to communicate your preferred hospital to the staff at Chippenham on arrival.
All straps applied, transport secured, and good peripheral motor strength confirmed in all extremities.
Final pulse before transport is 94 per minute, rest rates are stable at 20 breaths per minute.
Oxygen remains at 100 percent with NC. Breathing is significantly easier now.
We are ready to begin transport. No changes in condition noted.
